bytes.topic: Cross-Platform Trade-offs — what you gain vs what you give up vs going native
bytes.title: React Native - Cross-Platform Trade-Offs: When to Go Native
bytes.body: When building mobile apps with React Native, developers often face a trade-off between cross-platform development and native performance. While React Native provides a seamless experience across platforms, going native can offer better performance and access to device-specific features. To make an informed decision, consider the following: React Native's JavaScript bridge can introduce latency, but using Native Modules can mitigate this. However, native development requires learning platform-specific languages like Java or Swift, and managing separate codebases for each platform.

bytes.topic: Performance — JS thread vs UI thread in RN, jank, avoiding bridge overload
bytes.title: Avoiding Bridge Overload in React Native: JS Thread vs UI Thread
bytes.body: In React Native, UI components are rendered on the UI thread, while JavaScript logic runs on the JS thread. Bridge overload occurs when the JS thread sends too many updates to the UI thread, causing jank and slowing down the app. To mitigate this, use `AppState` to detect changes and update the UI only when necessary. For example, use `AppState.addEventListener('change', () => this.setState({ ... }))` to update the state only when the app state changes.

bytes.topic: Expo — managed vs bare workflow, EAS Build, EAS Update (OTA updates)
bytes.title: React Native: Choosing Between Expo Managed and Bare Workflows
bytes.body: When building React Native apps, you can opt for either the managed or bare workflow. Expo's managed workflow provides a seamless development experience, while the bare workflow offers more control. For production apps, consider using Expo's EAS Build for automated builds and EAS Update for over-the-air (OTA) updates. With EAS Build, create optimized builds for various platforms, and use EAS Update to push updates to your users. For example, to use EAS Update, initialize the Expo SDK, add the EAS Update package, and call `EASUpdate.checkForUpdateAsync()` to check for updates.

bytes.topic: Testing — React Native Testing Library, Flutter widget tests, Detox for E2E
bytes.title: Optimizing React Native Testing with React Native Testing Library
bytes.body: When testing React Native components, you'll often use the React Native Testing Library (RNTL) to ensure your UI is functioning as expected. RNTL provides a more straightforward API than Jest's built-in test utilities, making it easier to write and maintain tests. To maximize RNTL's efficiency, leverage its built-in `waitFor` function to wait for elements to render before attempting to interact with them. Additionally, consider using `fireEvent` to simulate user interactions, such as tapping or typing, to test your component's behavior under various scenarios.

bytes.topic: Code Sharing — sharing logic between web and mobile, monorepo strategies
bytes.title: React Native Monorepo Strategies for Code Sharing

bytes.topic: Over-the-Air Updates — EAS Update, CodePush, when OTA is and isn't safe
bytes.title: React Native: Choosing Between EAS Update, CodePush, and When OTA is Safe
bytes.body: When implementing Over-the-Air (OTA) updates for React Native apps, developers often face a dilemma between using Expo's EAS Update and Microsoft's CodePush. Both services provide seamless updates, but they have different use cases. EAS Update is ideal for apps built using Expo's managed workflow, whereas CodePush is better suited for apps built with React Native's bare workflow. Moreover, CodePush allows for more granular control over update deployment, making it a safer choice for apps with sensitive user data.
